0:0.rendering: In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.
0:0.note: The Hebrew word for "beginning" echoes the first word of the Bible, setting a stage that later biblical authors will repeatedly revisit as the story unfolds.

0:1.rendering: The earth was formless and empty, darkness was over the face of the deep, and the Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters.
0:1.note: The Hebrew word for "hovering" here is the same one used for an eagle gently fluttering over its young in Deuteronomy 32:11, painting the Spirit as a tender, life-giving presence amid chaos.

0:2.rendering: Then God said, "Let there be light," and there was light.
0:2.note: This first spoken word echoes the later creative power of God's word, as in Isaiah 55:11 where it accomplishes its purpose.

0:3.rendering: God saw the light, that it was good, and God separated the light from the darkness.
0:3.note: The word "saw" here echoes how a craftsman inspects his work, and "good" carries the sense of both beautiful and functional in ancient Hebrew.

0:4.rendering: God called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night. There was evening and there was morning, one day.
0:4.note: The Hebrew word order, "evening and morning," shows the ancient Israelite day beginning at sunset, a pattern echoed throughout the Torah.

0:5.rendering: Then God said, "Let there be a vault in the middle of the waters, and let it separate waters from waters."
0:5.note: The Hebrew word for vault (raqia) evokes something hammered out thin and spread wide, like a sheet of metal, mirroring how ancient skies were imagined as a solid dome holding back the upper waters.

0:6.rendering: So God made the vault and separated the waters under the vault from the waters above the vault. And it was so.
0:6.note: The word for vault (raqia) echoes the ancient idea of a hammered-out dome, holding back cosmic waters above us.

0:7.rendering: God called the vault 'sky.' There was evening and there was morning, a second day.
0:7.note: The Hebrew raqia, this hammered-out 'vault,' echoes ancient views of a solid dome holding back chaotic waters, yet here it is simply God's named workspace.

0:8.rendering: God said, "Let the waters under the heavens be gathered into one place, so that the dry land may appear." And it was so.
0:8.note: This gathering of waters echoes the ancient Near Eastern motif of separating chaotic seas to establish ordered creation.

0:9.rendering: God called the dry ground Land, and the gathered waters he called Seas. And God saw that it was good.
0:9.note: The Hebrew word for "gathered" (qawah) echoes the later command for Israel to "gather" at the mountain, hinting that creation itself anticipates covenant assembly.

0:10.rendering: Then God said, Let the land sprout with sprouting plants: seed-bearing herbs and fruit trees on the land that yield fruit according to their kinds, with their seed in them. And it was so.
0:10.note: The triple repetition of "seed" and "according to their kinds" echoes the ancient concern for ordered fertility in a world that once teemed with chaotic waters.

0:11.rendering: The land brought forth vegetation: plants that produce seed according to their kinds, and trees that bear fruit with seed in it according to their kinds. And God saw that it was good.
0:11.note: The repeated phrase "according to their kinds" echoes the order and fruitfulness of creation, setting a pattern that later biblical laws will echo in Israel's call to live distinctly.

0:12.rendering: Evening came, and morning came: the third day.
0:12.note: The repeated "evening and morning" refrain echoes the ancient pattern of the workday, marking each creative stage as complete and good before the next begins.

0:13.rendering: God said, "Let there be lights in the dome of the sky to separate the day from the night. Let them serve as signs for the set times, for days and for years."
0:13.note: The Hebrew word for "signs" here is the same one used for the rainbow after the flood and the blood on the doorposts at Passover, marking these lights as covenant reminders of God's ordered time.

0:14.rendering: And let them be for lights in the vault of the heavens to give light on the earth. And it was so.
0:14.note: The repeated phrase "and it was so" echoes the creative power of God's word, linking this day to the later command in Genesis 9:13 where God sets his bow in the clouds as a sign.

0:15.rendering: God made the two great lights, the greater light to rule the day and the lesser light to rule the night, and he made the stars also.
0:15.note: The Hebrew verb for "made" here echoes the same word used for the idols that nations craft in later texts, quietly dethroning the sun and moon from divine status in the ancient Near East.

0:16.rendering: God set them in the vault of the heavens to give light on the earth.
0:16.note: The Hebrew verb for "set" echoes the same word used for planting the garden in Eden, hinting that the skies are a cosmic garden tended by the Creator.

0:17.rendering: God set them in the vault of the heavens to give light on the earth, to rule over the day and over the night, and to separate the light from the darkness. And God saw that it was good.
0:17.note: The sun and moon are appointed as humble governors, not rival deities, echoing ancient Near Eastern creation stories while firmly dethroning them.

0:18.rendering: There was evening and there was morning, the fourth day.
0:18.note: This rhythmic refrain echoes the ancient pattern of the workweek, marking the sun, moon, and stars as timekeepers for worship and seasons.

0:19.rendering: Then God said, "Let the waters swarm with swarms of living creatures, and let birds fly above the earth across the face of the sky."
0:19.note: The Hebrew verb "swarm" (sharats) echoes the teeming chaos of the waters in Genesis 1:2, now ordered into life by God's word.

0:20.rendering: So God created the great sea monsters and every living creature that moves, with which the waters swarm, according to their kinds, and every winged bird according to its kind. And God saw that it was good.
0:20.note: The Hebrew word for "sea monsters" (tanninim) echoes the chaotic dragons of ancient Near Eastern myths, yet here they are simply creatures under God's effortless command.

0:21.rendering: God blessed them and said, "Be fruitful and multiply, fill the waters in the seas, and let birds multiply on the earth."
0:21.note: This first command to flourish echoes God's own creative act, inviting sea creatures and birds to partner in filling what he has shaped.

0:22.rendering: There was evening and there was morning, a fifth day.
0:22.note: The repeated refrain "evening and morning" echoes the ancient pattern of the day beginning in darkness, inviting us to see God's ordering of time as a rhythmic gift rather than our modern clock-driven rush.

0:23.rendering: Then God said, "Let the earth bring forth living creatures according to their kinds: livestock, creeping things, and beasts of the earth according to their kinds." And it was so.
0:23.note: The Hebrew word for "bring forth" echoes the land's earlier sprouting of vegetation in verse 11, showing the earth itself participates as a partner in God's creative work.

0:24.rendering: God made the wild animals of the earth after their kinds, the livestock after their kinds, and everything that creeps on the ground after its kind. And God saw that it was good.
0:24.note: The triple repetition of "after their kinds" echoes the ordered, teeming diversity of Genesis 1, quietly contrasting the chaotic mixing of creatures in ancient Near Eastern myths.

0:25.rendering: Then God said, "Let us make humankind in our image, according to our likeness, and let them rule over the fish of the sea, over the birds of the sky, over the livestock, over all the earth, and over every creeping thing that creeps on the earth."
0:25.note: The surprising plural "us" and "our" echoes the heavenly court in ancient Near Eastern texts, pulling humanity into the divine conversation.

0:26.rendering: So God created the human in his image, in the image of God he created him, male and female he created them.
0:26.note: The Hebrew wordplay between 'adam (human) and 'adamah (ground) shows God forming a single representative creature that is then differentiated into male and female, both bearing the divine image together.

0:27.rendering: God blessed them and said to them, "Be fruitful and multiply, fill the earth and subdue it, and rule over the fish of the sea, the birds of the sky, and every living thing that moves on the earth."
0:27.note: This echoes the ancient royal commission to image-bearing stewards, setting humanity's vocation within the cosmic temple God has just spoken into being.

0:28.rendering: God said, "Look, I give you every plant that bears seed on the face of all the earth, and every tree with fruit that bears seed in it. They shall be yours for food."
0:28.note: The Hebrew repeats "seed-bearing" three times, echoing God's own life-giving creativity now handed to humanity.

0:29.rendering: And to every beast of the earth, and to every bird of the heavens, and to everything that creeps on the earth in which there is the breath of life, I have given every green plant for food. And it was so.
0:29.note: The same breath of life that animates humans also fills the animals, making the whole living world a single interdependent community sustained by God's provision.

0:30.rendering: God saw everything that he had made, and behold, it was very good. There was evening and there was morning, the sixth day.
0:30.note: The Hebrew "very good" (tov me'od) upgrades the repeated "good" of prior days, hinting at the completed harmony of creation before the fall.

1:0.rendering: So the heavens and the earth and all their vast array were finished.
1:0.note: The word "finished" echoes the creation refrain "and it was so," signaling the ancient view of the cosmos as a completed, ordered temple ready for its divine inhabitant.

1:1.rendering: And on the seventh day God finished his work that he had done, and he rested on the seventh day from all his work that he had done.
1:1.note: The Hebrew verb for "rested" (shabat) is the root of "Sabbath," echoing how God will later call Israel to imitate this rhythm of completion and delight.

1:2.rendering: God blessed the seventh day and made it holy, because on it he rested from all his work that he had created and made.
1:2.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "rested" (shabat) to "Sabbath" (shabbat), marking this day as the first thing God declares holy in the story.

1:3.rendering: These are the generations of the heavens and the earth when they were created, in the day that the LORD God made earth and heavens.
1:3.note: This heading uses the key Hebrew word "toledot" (generations), stitching Genesis together like chapter markers while echoing the creation language from chapter 1.

1:4.rendering: Now no bush of the field was yet on the earth, and no plant of the field had yet sprung up, for the LORD God had not caused it to rain on the land, and there was no human to work the ground.
1:4.note: The Hebrew word for "human" (adam) echoes the "ground" (adamah), hinting that humanity and soil are intertwined from the start.

1:5.rendering: But a mist would rise up from the earth and water the whole face of the ground.
1:5.note: This mist, or 'ed in Hebrew, echoes the ancient Near Eastern idea of subterranean waters surging upward, setting the stage for the garden's lush life before rain enters the story.

1:6.rendering: Yahweh God shaped the human from the dust of the ground, breathed the breath of life into his nostrils, and the human became a living being.
1:6.note: The wordplay between adam (human) and adamah (ground) roots us in the earth even as God's breath lifts us into life.

1:7.rendering: Yahweh God planted a garden in Eden, in the east, and there he put the human he had formed.
1:7.note: The word "planted" echoes how God "made" the earth sprout vegetation in Genesis 1:11-12, showing the garden as the first cultivated space where human life begins.

1:8.rendering: And Yahweh God made all kinds of trees grow from the ground, delightful to look at and good to eat, including the tree of life in the middle of the garden and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.
1:8.note: The two central trees form the garden's axis, echoing the later choice between life and wisdom in Israel's story.

1:9.rendering: A river flowed out of Eden to water the garden, and from there it divided and became four headwaters.
1:9.note: The single river splitting into four echoes the ancient Near Eastern idea of paradise as the source of the world's life-giving waters.

1:10.rendering: The name of the first is Pishon. It is the one that winds through the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold.
1:10.note: The name Pishon, meaning "to increase" or "to spread," echoes the river's winding path and hints at the abundance it brings to Havilah's gold-rich soil.

1:11.rendering: And the gold of that land is good. There is also bdellium and the onyx stone.
1:11.note: The Hebrew word for "good" here echoes the repeated "good" of creation days, hinting that this gold and gem-rich land outside Eden still carries echoes of the Creator's original delight.

1:12.rendering: The name of the second river is the Gihon. It winds through the whole land of Cush.
1:12.note: The Hebrew verb for "winds through" is the same one used for the twisting, coiling motion of a snake, hinting at the subtle danger already present in the good creation.

1:13.rendering: The name of the third river is the Tigris, which flows east of Assyria. And the fourth river is the Euphrates.
1:13.note: These two rivers bookend the known world of the ancient Near East, quietly framing Eden as the source of life for the empires yet to come.

1:14.rendering: Yahweh God took the human and settled him in the garden of Eden to work it and to watch over it.
1:14.note: The Hebrew word for "settled him" echoes the later promise that God would settle his people in their land, hinting that Eden is the first sanctuary where humans serve as priestly caretakers.

1:15.rendering: And the LORD God commanded the man, saying, "From every tree of the garden you may freely eat."
1:15.note: The Hebrew verb for "commanded" carries both permission and prohibition in the same breath, setting the stage for the single limit that will test trust.

1:16.rendering: but from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat, for on the day you eat from it you will surely die.
1:16.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "knowledge" (da'at) and "die" (mot tamut) hints at a profound reversal of life through grasping at wisdom on our own terms.

1:17.rendering: Then the LORD God said, It is not good for the human to be alone. I will make him a helper corresponding to him.
1:17.note: The Hebrew word for helper often describes God himself coming to rescue, and corresponding to him carries the idea of a mirror-image counterpart.

1:18.rendering: So the LORD God formed from the ground every beast of the field and every bird of the heavens, and brought them to the man to see what he would call them. Whatever the man called each living creature, that was its name.
1:18.note: The same Hebrew verb for God "forming" the animals here echoes his "forming" the man in verse 7, linking them in shared earthy origins while the man now exercises royal naming authority.

1:19.rendering: The man gave names to all the livestock, to the birds of the sky, and to every wild animal. But for the man himself no suitable helper was found.
1:19.note: The Hebrew word for "helper" here is the same one used for God as Israel's rescuer, hinting that the needed partner is neither inferior nor superior, but a matching counterpart.

1:20.rendering: So the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall on the man, and while he slept he took one of his ribs and closed up its place with flesh.
1:20.note: The Hebrew word for "rib" also means "side," hinting that the woman comes from the man's very side, not his head or foot, for equality in their shared humanity.

1:21.rendering: And the LORD God built the rib that he had taken from the man into a woman, and he brought her to the man.
1:21.note: The Hebrew verb "built" echoes the construction language of the tabernacle later in the Torah, hinting that the woman is a sacred space where God's presence will dwell with humanity.

1:22.rendering: The man said, "This one at last is bone from my bones and flesh from my flesh. She shall be called woman, for from man she was taken."
1:22.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "man" (ish) and "woman" (ishah) highlights their profound unity while honoring their distinction.

1:23.rendering: That is why a man leaves his father and his mother, clings to his wife, and the two of them become one flesh.
1:23.note: The Hebrew word for "clings" is the same one used for Israel cleaving to God, turning marriage into a picture of covenant loyalty.

1:24.rendering: The two of them were naked, the man and his wife, and they felt no shame.
1:24.note: In the ancient Near East nakedness often signaled vulnerability or humiliation, yet here it marks perfect trust and harmony before any fracture.

3:0.rendering: Now the man knew Eve his wife, and she conceived and gave birth to Cain. She said, "I have acquired a man with the Lord."
3:0.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "acquired" (qaniti) and the name Cain hints at Eve's bold hope that this child might somehow fulfill the promise of Genesis 3:15.

3:1.rendering: Later she gave birth to his brother Abel. Abel became a keeper of flocks, but Cain was a worker of the ground.
3:1.note: The Hebrew wordplay is sharp: Abel means "breath" or "futility," while Cain's name echoes "acquired," setting up the tragic irony of the one who seems substantial becoming the murderer.

3:2.rendering: In the course of time Cain brought some of the fruit of the ground as an offering to the LORD.
3:2.note: The Hebrew "at the end of days" hints this was a set time, perhaps the first harvest festival, echoing later temple rhythms.

3:3.rendering: And Abel, for his part, brought from the firstborn of his flock and from their fat portions. And the LORD looked with favor on Abel and on his offering.
3:3.note: The word for "looked with favor" is the same one used for God "regarding" or "seeing" in the flood story and the exodus, hinting that divine delight is already choosing unlikely paths through the younger brother.

3:4.rendering: But for Cain and for his offering he had no regard. So Cain became very angry, and his face fell.
3:4.note: The same Hebrew verb for "regard" links God's favor here to the "respect" shown to Abel in verse 4, heightening the sting of divine preference.

3:5.rendering: The Lord said to Cain, "Why are you angry, and why has your face fallen?"
3:5.note: This divine question echoes God's earlier one to Adam in the garden, probing the heart before judgment falls.

3:6.rendering: If you do well, will you not be lifted up? But if you do not do well, sin is crouching at the door, its desire is for you, yet you can rule over it.
3:6.note: The word for "crouching" echoes the same verb used of the serpent in Genesis 3, hinting that the beast outside the door is the same ancient enemy now waiting to pounce.

3:7.rendering: Cain said to his brother Abel, "Let's go out to the field." And when they were in the field, Cain rose up against his brother Abel and killed him.
3:7.note: The Hebrew literally says Cain "rose up" against Abel, the same verb used when God "raised up" humanity from the dust, twisting creation's upward movement into murder.

3:8.rendering: Then the LORD said to Cain, "Where is your brother Abel?" He said, "I don't know. Am I my brother's keeper?"
3:8.note: Cain's evasive question twists the ancient cultural duty of a brother to protect and guard his sibling.

3:9.rendering: The LORD said, "What have you done? The voice of your brother's blood is crying out to me from the ground."
3:9.note: The Hebrew word for "blood" here is plural, "bloods," evoking not just one life but all the potential generations Abel would never father.

3:10.rendering: Now you are cursed from the ground, which has opened its mouth to take your brother's blood from your hand.
3:10.note: The ground that drank Abel's blood now rejects Cain, echoing the curse on Adam but intensified.

3:11.rendering: When you work the ground, it will no longer yield its strength to you. You will be a restless wanderer on the earth.
3:11.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "Cain" and "qaniti" (I have acquired) echoes in the curse that the ground will no longer "yield" (literally "give") its strength, turning his violent taking into endless restless exile.

3:12.rendering: Cain said to the LORD, My punishment is greater than I can bear.
3:12.note: The Hebrew word for "punishment" is the same as for "iniquity," linking Cain's guilt and its crushing consequences in one loaded term.

3:13.rendering: Look, today you have driven me from the face of the ground. I will be hidden from your face. I will become a restless wanderer on the earth, and anyone who finds me will kill me.
3:13.note: The Hebrew word for "restless wanderer" (nad) echoes the earlier curse on Cain to be "driven" from the soil, turning his punishment into a life of perpetual, anxious motion.

3:14.rendering: Then the LORD said to him, "Therefore, if anyone kills Cain, sevenfold vengeance will be taken on him." And the LORD put a mark on Cain, so that no one who found him would strike him down.
3:14.note: The "sevenfold" vengeance echoes the poetic justice in Lamech's later boast, showing how God's protective mark subverts human cycles of escalating revenge.

3:15.rendering: So Cain went away from the face of the LORD, and he settled in the land of Nod, east of Eden.
3:15.note: Nod means "wandering," a wordplay that turns Cain's restless exile into the very name of his new home.

3:16.rendering: Cain knew his wife, she conceived and gave birth to Enoch. He was building a city, and he called the name of the city after the name of his son, Enoch.
3:16.note: The first city in the Bible is founded by a murderer and named after his son, echoing the ancient pattern of rulers building monuments to secure their legacy.

3:17.rendering: To Enoch was born Irad, and Irad fathered Mehujael, and Mehujael fathered Methushael, and Methushael fathered Lamech.
3:17.note: This tight genealogy in Cain's line quietly echoes the ten-generation structure of Seth's in chapter 5, hinting at parallel human stories unfolding side by side.

3:18.rendering: Lamech took two wives for himself: the name of the first was Adah, and the name of the second was Zillah.
3:18.note: The first polygamous marriage recorded in Scripture, echoing the fractured human attempt to seize blessing and security apart from God's design.

3:19.rendering: Adah gave birth to Jabal. He was the father of those who live in tents and raise livestock.
3:19.note: The first mention of a "father" in the Bible launches human culture through specialization, echoing the creative mandate to fill and shape the earth.

3:20.rendering: His brother's name was Jubal. He was the father of all who play the lyre and the pipe.
3:20.note: Jubal's name echoes the Hebrew word for 'ram's horn' or 'trumpet,' hinting that music itself was born in the line of Cain.

3:21.rendering: Zillah also gave birth to Tubal-cain, who forged all kinds of bronze and iron tools. Tubal-cain's sister was Naamah.
3:21.note: The first metalsmith emerges right after the first city-builder, showing how quickly human skill turns raw creation into shaped tools.

3:22.rendering: Lamech said to his wives: Adah and Zillah, hear my voice, you wives of Lamech, give ear to what I say. I have killed a man for wounding me, a young man for striking me.
3:22.note: Lamech's boastful poem twists the earlier "eye for an eye" principle into violent excess, echoing and inverting the Cain story.

3:23.rendering: If Cain is avenged seven times, then Lamech seventy-seven times.
3:23.note: Lamech twists God's mercy into a license for limitless revenge, escalating the violence that began with his ancestor Cain.

3:24.rendering: Adam knew his wife again, and she bore a son and called his name Seth, for she said, "God has appointed for me another offspring instead of Abel, for Cain killed him."
3:24.note: Eve's wordplay on "Seth" (from Hebrew shat, "appointed") echoes her earlier pain in naming Cain, turning grief into quiet hope for renewed seed.

3:25.rendering: To Seth also a son was born, and he called his name Enosh. At that time people began to call on the name of the Lord.
3:25.note: The name Enosh means "human one," echoing the frailty first named in Genesis 2-3, and here marks the moment humanity first openly invokes Yahweh's personal name.

2:0.rendering: Now the snake was more shrewd than any beast of the field that the LORD God had made, and it said to the woman, "Did God really say, 'You must not eat from any tree of the garden'?"
2:0.note: The Hebrew word for "shrewd" (arum) echoes the "naked" (arumim) of 2:25, hinting that this clever creature will expose their vulnerability.

2:1.rendering: The woman said to the serpent, "We may eat fruit from the trees of the garden."
2:1.note: The woman quotes God's exact words back to the serpent, already tightening the command from "every tree" to just "the trees of the garden" before the serpent even replies.

2:2.rendering: but about the fruit of the tree in the middle of the garden, God said, 'You shall not eat from it, and you shall not touch it, or you will die.'
2:2.note: Eve adds the words "and you shall not touch it," an expansion that echoes the serpent's later twisting of God's exact command.

2:3.rendering: The serpent said to the woman, "You will not surely die."
2:3.note: The serpent's words twist God's exact phrasing from 2:17, replacing the emphatic warning with a flat denial that plants the first seeds of doubt.

2:4.rendering: For God knows that when you eat from it your eyes will be opened, and you will be like God, knowing good and evil.
2:4.note: The serpent's promise twists the ancient idea of divine wisdom into self-exalting autonomy, echoing the very knowledge God possesses yet offering it as a shortcut apart from trust.

2:5.rendering: The woman saw that the tree was good for food, that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was desirable to make one wise, so she took some of its fruit and ate. She also gave some to her husband who was with her, and he ate.
2:5.note: The three adjectives echo God's own sevenfold declaration that creation was "good" in Genesis 1, but now the woman reclassifies the forbidden tree through her own eyes.

2:6.rendering: Then the eyes of both of them were opened, and they knew that they were naked. So they sewed fig leaves together and made loincloths for themselves.
2:6.note: Their eyes are "opened" with the very knowledge the serpent promised, yet it brings shame instead of godlike wisdom, an early echo of how twisted desire distorts sight.

2:7.rendering: They heard the sound of the Lord God walking in the garden in the cool of the day, and the man and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the Lord God among the trees of the garden.
2:7.note: The word for "walking" here is the same one used later for God "walking about" in the tabernacle, hinting that Eden itself was the first sanctuary.

2:8.rendering: But the LORD God called to the man and said to him, "Where are you?"
2:8.note: This first question in the Bible is not about location but about the man's new reality of hiding and fear after his eyes were opened.

2:9.rendering: He replied, "I heard the sound of you walking in the garden, and I was afraid because I am naked, so I hid myself."
2:9.note: The man's answer echoes the earlier "voice" of the serpent and the "sound" of God's approach, showing how fear now warps the once-intimate walk with God.

2:10.rendering: And he said, Who told you that you were naked? Have you eaten from the tree that I commanded you not to eat from?
2:10.note: The question "Who told you?" echoes the serpent's earlier claim to give knowledge, exposing how the forbidden fruit delivered shame instead of wisdom.

2:11.rendering: The man said, "The woman whom you gave to be with me, she gave me some of the tree's fruit, and I ate."
2:11.note: Adam's first recorded words after the fall deflect blame onto both the woman and God who gave her, echoing the serpent's earlier twist on God's words.

2:12.rendering: Yahweh God said to the woman, "What is this you have done?" The woman said, "The serpent deceived me, and I ate."
2:12.note: The Hebrew word for "deceived" here echoes the serpent's own craftiness in verse 1, showing how its cunning looped back on the woman.

2:13.rendering: Then the LORD God said to the snake, Because you have done this, cursed are you above all the livestock and above all the beasts of the field. On your belly you shall go, and dust you shall eat all the days of your life.
2:13.note: The curse uses the same Hebrew verb for "cursed" that will later fall on the ground itself, linking the snake's humiliation to the soil it now must eat.

2:14.rendering: I will put hostility between you and the woman, between your offspring and her offspring. He will strike your head, and you will strike his heel.
2:14.note: This first whisper of enmity sets up a long biblical thread of conflict between two seeds, one that echoes from here through the prophets to the cross.

2:15.rendering: To the woman he said, "I will greatly increase your pain in childbearing, in pain you will bring forth children. Your desire will be for your husband, and he will rule over you."
2:15.note: The Hebrew word for "pain" here echoes the "toil" pronounced over the man in the next verse, linking their shared curse to the ground's resistance.

2:16.rendering: To the man he said, "Because you listened to the voice of your wife and ate from the tree that I commanded you not to eat from, cursed is the ground because of you. In painful toil you will eat from it all the days of your life."
2:16.note: The ground, once freely yielding, now fights back with thorns, linking human rebellion to creation's frustration in a single Hebrew word for "curse."

2:17.rendering: It will sprout thorns and thistles for you, and you will eat the plants of the field.
2:17.note: This curse directly echoes the ground's original blessing in Genesis 1:11-12, twisting fruitful soil into a place of painful labor.

2:18.rendering: By the sweat of your face you will eat bread until you return to the ground, for out of it you were taken. For you are dust, and to dust you shall return.
2:18.note: The Hebrew wordplay between adam (human) and adamah (ground) ties our fate to the very soil we were formed from.

2:19.rendering: The man called his wife's name Eve, because she was the mother of all living.
2:19.note: The name Eve plays on the Hebrew word for "living" (chavah), a wordplay that turns the first death sentence into a defiant promise of life.

2:20.rendering: Yahweh God made garments of skins for Adam and his wife, and he clothed them.
2:20.note: This first act of divine clothing after the curse echoes forward through the Bible, from priestly robes to the seamless tunic of Jesus.

2:21.rendering: Then the LORD God said, "Look, the man has become like one of us, knowing good and evil. What if he now reaches out his hand, takes from the tree of life also, eats, and lives forever?"
2:21.note: The phrase "like one of us" echoes the plural "let us make" in Genesis 1:26, hinting at divine council language in the ancient Near East.

2:22.rendering: So the LORD God sent him out from the garden of Eden to work the ground from which he had been taken.
2:22.note: The Hebrew word for "sent him out" carries the force of driving away or banishing, echoing the same verb used for casting out Cain in the next chapter.

2:23.rendering: So he drove out the human, and stationed east of the garden of Eden the cherubim and the whirling sword of flame to guard the way to the tree of life.
2:23.note: The whirling, flaming sword echoes the fiery theophanies of Sinai and the temple's cherubim guardians, turning humanity's first home into a guarded sanctuary no one can re-enter on their own.
